content:
# Exodus Chapter 25 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~46v–47r (PDF pp. 91–92); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 67–68 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_025_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_025_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_025_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_025_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Offering for the Sanctuary (vv. 1–9)

**1** And YHWH [**YHWH**] spoke [**vayedabber**] to [**el**] Moses [**Mosheh**] saying [**le'mor**],

**2** "Speak [**dabber**] to [**el**] the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**] and let them take [**veyiqchu**] for Me [**li**] an offering [**terumah**]. From [**me'et**] every [**kol**] man [**ish**] whose [**asher**] heart [**libbo**] moves him [**yiddevennu**] you shall take [**tiqchu**] My offering [**et-terumati**].

**3** And this [**vezot**] is the offering [**hatterumah**] which [**asher**] you shall take [**tiqchu**] from them [**me'ittam**]: gold [**zahav**] and silver [**vakhesef**] and bronze [**unechoshet**],

**4** and blue [**utekheleth**] and purple [**ve'argaman**] and scarlet yarn [**vetola'at shani**] and fine linen [**veshesh**] and goats' hair [**ve'izzim**],

**5** and rams' [**ve'orot eylim**] skins [**orot**] dyed red [**me'oddamim**], and tahash [**ve'orot techashim**] skins, and acacia [**va'atsey**] wood [**shittim**],

**6** oil [**shemen**] for the light [**lamma'or**], spices [**besamim**] for the anointing [**leshemen hammishchah**] oil [**shemen**] and for the fragrant [**veliqqtoret**] incense [**hassammim**],

**7** onyx [**avney-shoham**] stones [**avney**] and setting [**ve'avney**] stones [**millu'im**] for the ephod [**la'efod**] and for the breastpiece [**velachoshen**].

**8** And let them make [**ve'asu**] for Me [**li**] a sanctuary [**miqdash**], and I will dwell [**veshakhanti**] in their midst [**betokham**].

**9** According to all [**kekhol**] that [**asher**] I [**ani**] am showing [**mar'eh**] you [**otekha**] — the pattern [**et tavnit**] of the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**] and the pattern [**ve'et tavnit**] of all [**kol**] its vessels [**kelav**] — so [**vekhen**] you shall make [**ta'asu**] it."

---

## The Ark of the Testimony (vv. 10–22)

**10** "And they shall make [**ve'asu**] an ark [**aron**] of acacia [**atsey**] wood [**shittim**]: two cubits [**ammatayim**] and a half [**vachetsi**] its length [**orkho**], and a cubit [**ve'ammah**] and a half [**vachetsi**] its width [**rochbo**], and a cubit [**ve'ammah**] and a half [**vachetsi**] its height [**qomato**].

**11** And you shall overlay [**vetsippita**] it [**oto**] with pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**]; from inside [**mibbayyit**] and from outside [**umichutz**] you shall overlay it [**tetsappennu**], and you shall make [**ve'asita**] upon it [**alav**] a rim [**zer**] of gold [**zahav**] all around [**saviv**].

**12** And you shall cast [**veyatsaqta**] for it [**lo**] four [**arba**] rings [**tabb'ot**] of gold [**zahav**] and put them [**venatattha**] on [**al**] its four [**arba**] feet [**pa'amotav**], and two [**ushtey**] rings [**tabba'ot**] on [**al**] its one [**tsal'o**] side [**ha'echat**] and two [**ushtey**] rings [**tabba'ot**] on [**al**] its other [**tsal'o**] side [**hasshenit**].

**13** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] poles [**baddey**] of acacia [**atsey**] wood [**shittim**] and overlay [**vetsippita**] them [**otam**] with gold [**zahav**].

**14** And you shall put [**veheve'ta**] the poles [**et-habbaddim**] into the rings [**battabba'ot**] on the sides [**al tsal'ot**] of the ark [**ha'aron**] to carry [**laset**] the ark [**et-ha'aron**] by them [**bahem**].

**15** In the rings [**betabb'ot**] of the ark [**ha'aron**] the poles [**habbaddim**] shall be [**yihyu**]; they shall not be removed [**lo yasuru**] from it [**mimmennu**].

**16** And you shall put [**venatattha**] into [**el**] the ark [**ha'aron**] the testimony [**et-ha'edut**] which [**asher**] I shall give [**etten**] to you [**elekha**].

**17** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] a mercy seat [**kapporet**] of pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**]: two cubits [**ammatayim**] and a half [**vachetsi**] its length [**orkah**] and a cubit [**ve'ammah**] and a half [**vachetsi**] its width [**rochbah**].

**18** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] two [**shneyim**] cherubim [**keruvim**] of gold [**zahav**]; of hammered work [**miqshah**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] them [**otam**], from the two [**mishshney**] ends [**qetsot**] of the mercy seat [**hakkapporet**].

**19** And make [**va'aseh**] one [**keruv echad**] cherub [**keruv**] from this [**miqatseh**] end [**mizzeh**] and one [**ukeruv-echad**] cherub [**keruv**] from that [**miqatseh**] end [**mizzeh**]; from [**min**] the mercy seat [**hakkapporet**] you shall make [**ta'asu**] the cherubim [**et-hakkeruvim**] on its two [**al-shney**] ends [**qetsotav**].

**20** And the cherubim [**vehayu hakkeruvim**] shall be spreading [**porsey**] their wings [**khenafayim**] upward [**lema'lah**], covering [**sokhekhim**] with their wings [**bekhanfeyhem**] over [**al**] the mercy seat [**hakkapporet**], and their faces [**ufeneyhem**] each toward the other [**ish el-achiv**]; toward [**el**] the mercy seat [**hakkapporet**] shall be [**yihyu**] the faces [**peney**] of the cherubim [**hakkeruvim**].

**21** And you shall put [**venatattha**] the mercy seat [**et-hakkapporet**] on top of [**al**] the ark [**ha'aron**] from above [**milma'lah**], and into [**ve'el**] the ark [**ha'aron**] you shall put [**titten**] the testimony [**et-ha'edut**] which [**asher**] I shall give [**etten**] to you [**elekha**].

**22** And I will meet with you [**veno'adti**] there [**lekha sham**], and I will speak [**vedibbarthi**] with you [**ittekha**] from above [**me'al**] the mercy seat [**hakkapporet**], from between [**mibbein**] the two [**shney**] cherubim [**hakkeruvim**] which [**asher**] are upon [**al**] the ark [**aron**] of the testimony [**ha'edut**], concerning all [**et kol**] that [**asher**] I will command [**atsavveh**] you [**otekha**] for [**el**] the Sons of Israel [**beney Yisra'el**]."

---

## The Table of the Bread of the Presence (vv. 23–30)

**23** "And you shall make [**ve'asita**] a table [**shulchan**] of acacia [**atsey**] wood [**shittim**]: two cubits [**ammatayim**] its length [**orkho**] and a cubit [**ve'ammah**] its width [**rochbo**] and a cubit [**ve'ammah**] and a half [**vachetsi**] its height [**qomato**].

**24** And you shall overlay [**vetsippita**] it [**oto**] with pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**] and make [**ve'asita**] for it [**lo**] a rim [**zer**] of gold [**zahav**] all around [**saviv**].

**25** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] for it [**lo**] a frame [**misgeret**] a handbreadth [**tofach**] wide all around [**saviv**], and you shall make [**ve'asita**] a rim [**zer**] of gold [**zahav**] for its frame [**lemisgaretto**] all around [**saviv**].

**26** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] for it [**lo**] four [**arba**] rings [**tabb'ot**] of gold [**zahav**] and put [**venatattha**] the rings [**et-hattabba'ot**] on [**al**] the four [**arba**] corners [**happi'ot**] which [**asher**] are at its four [**le'arba**] legs [**raglav**].

**27** Close to [**le'ummat**] the frame [**hammisgeret**] the rings [**hattabb'ot**] shall be [**tihyeynah**], as holders [**lebattim**] for the poles [**labaddim**] to carry [**laset**] the table [**et-hashulchan**].

**28** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] the poles [**et-habbaddim**] of acacia [**atsey**] wood [**shittim**] and overlay [**vetsippita**] them [**otam**] with gold [**zahav**], and the table [**hashulchan**] shall be carried [**venissa**] with them [**bam**].

**29** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] its plates [**qe'arotav**] and its ladles [**vekhappotav**] and its pitchers [**uqesotav**] and its bowls [**umenaqiyyotav**] with which [**asher**] it will be poured out [**yussakh**]; of pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] them [**otam**].

**30** And you shall set [**venatattha**] on [**al**] the table [**hashulchan**] the bread of the Presence [**lechem panim**] before Me [**lefanay**] continually [**tamid**]."

---

## The Lampstand of Pure Gold (vv. 31–40)

**31** "And you shall make [**ve'asita**] a lampstand [**menorat**] of pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**]; of hammered work [**miqshah**] the lampstand [**hammenorah**] shall be made [**te'aseh**]. Its base [**yerekhah**] and its shaft [**veqanah**], its cups [**gevi'eha**], its buds [**kaftoreha**] and its blossoms [**ufracheha**] — from it [**mimmennah**] they shall be [**yihyu**].

**32** And six [**veshishah**] branches [**qanim**] going out [**yotse'im**] from its sides [**mitsiddeyha**]: three [**shloshah**] branches [**qeney**] of the lampstand [**menorah**] from its one [**mitsiddah**] side [**ha'echad**] and three [**ushloshah**] branches [**qeney**] of the lampstand [**menorah**] from its other [**mitsiddah**] side [**hassheni**].

**33** Three [**shloshah**] cups [**gevi'im**] shaped like almond blossoms [**meshukqadim**] on the one [**ha'echad**] branch [**baqqaneh**], a bud [**kaftor**] and a blossom [**vaferach**]; and three [**ushloshah**] cups [**gevi'im**] shaped like almond blossoms [**meshukqadim**] on the other [**ha'echad**] branch [**baqqaneh**], a bud [**kaftor**] and a blossom [**vaferach**] — so [**ken**] for the six [**lesheshet**] branches [**haqqanim**] going out [**hayyotse'im**] from [**min**] the lampstand [**hammenorah**].

**34** And on the lampstand [**ubammenorah**] four [**arba'ah**] cups [**gevi'im**] shaped like almond blossoms [**meshukqadim**], its buds [**kaftoreha**] and its blossoms [**ufracheha**].

**35** And a bud [**vekaftor**] under [**tachat**] the two [**shney**] branches [**haqqanim**] from it [**mimmennu**], and a bud [**vekaftor**] under [**tachat**] the two [**shney**] branches [**haqqanim**] from it [**mimmennu**], and a bud [**vekaftor**] under [**tachat**] the two [**shney**] branches [**haqqanim**] from it [**mimmennu**] — for the six [**lesheshet**] branches [**haqqanim**] going out [**hayyotse'im**] from [**min**] the lampstand [**hammenorah**].

**36** Their buds [**kaftoreyhem**] and their branches [**uqenotam**] shall be [**yihyu**] from it [**mimmennah**] — all of it [**kullahh**] one [**echad**] hammered piece [**miqshah**] of pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**].

**37** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] its lamps [**et-neroteyha**] seven [**shiv'ah**], and he shall set up [**vehe'elah**] its lamps [**et-neroteyha**] and give light [**vehe'ir**] upon [**al**] the area before it [**ever paneyha**].

**38** And its tongs [**umalqacheyha**] and its fire pans [**umachtoteyha**] of pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**].

**39** Of a talent [**kikkar**] of pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**] he shall make [**ya'aseh**] it [**otah**] with all [**et kol**] these [**ha'eleh**] vessels [**hakkelim**].

**40** And see [**ure'eh**] and make [**va'aseh**] them according to their pattern [**betavnitam**] which [**asher**] you [**attah**] are being shown [**mar'eh**] on the mountain [**bahar**]."

---

## Translator's Notes

### Key Terms

**terumah** (vv. 2–3) -- "offering, contribution." The noun terumah derives from the Hiphil of r-w-m ("to raise, lift up"), and thus literally means "that which is raised up" or "an uplifted portion." It denotes a voluntary contribution set apart from one's possessions and elevated to a sacred purpose. The term is distinct from other offering words: it is not a sacrifice (zevach) placed on an altar, nor a tribute (mas) extracted by compulsion, but a free-will contribution motivated by the heart. The qualifying phrase asher yiddevennu libbo ("whose heart moves him") underscores this voluntary character -- the terumah is an act of the willing heart (lev nadiv), not a tax. The same principle will be restated more expansively in 35:5, 21-29, where the actual collection takes place. The LXX renders terumah as aparchē ("first-fruit, first-portion") in some contexts, but here in Vaticanus the term aparchē is used, emphasizing the offering as a dedication of the first and best.

**miqdash** (v. 8) -- "sanctuary, holy place." The noun is formed from the root q-d-sh ("to be holy, set apart") with the mem-preformative indicating a place: "a place of holiness." This is the first occurrence of the term in the Torah and the first divine command to build a physical dwelling for God's presence. The purpose clause veshakhanti betokham ("and I will dwell in their midst") is theologically momentous: the verb shakan ("to dwell, tabernacle") is the root from which mishkan ("Tabernacle") and later Shekhinah ("divine indwelling presence") derive. Notably, the text says betokham ("in their midst"), not betokhó ("in its midst") -- God's stated purpose is not merely to inhabit a structure but to dwell among His people. The building is the means; the relational presence is the end. The LXX renders miqdash as hagiasma ("holy place, sanctuary"), and veshakhanti betokham as kai ophthēsomai en humin ("and I will be seen/appear among you") -- a significant interpretive shift from "dwelling" to "appearing," perhaps reflecting a more transcendent theology of divine presence.

**tavnit** (vv. 9, 40) -- "pattern, plan, model." The noun tavnit (from b-n-h, "to build") denotes the architectural plan or design template shown to Moses on the mountain. The term appears at both the beginning (v. 9) and the end (v. 40) of the chapter, forming an inclusio that frames the entire Tabernacle instruction: everything must be built according to the heavenly pattern. The theological implication is that the earthly Tabernacle is a copy of a celestial archetype -- a concept developed extensively in Hebrews 8:5, which quotes this very verse: "See that you make everything according to the pattern (tupon) shown you on the mountain." The LXX renders tavnit as paradeigma ("pattern, model, example") in v. 9, the term from which English "paradigm" derives. The author of Hebrews uses tupos ("type, figure, pattern"), a near-synonym emphasizing the copy-to-original relationship between earthly and heavenly sanctuaries.

**kapporet** (vv. 17–22) -- "mercy seat, atonement cover." The noun kapporet derives from the root k-p-r ("to cover, atone, make propitiation") and denotes the solid gold lid placed atop the Ark of the Testimony. The kapporet is not merely a cover but the focal point of atonement: on Yom Kippur the high priest will sprinkle blood upon it and before it (Lev 16:14-15). It is also the locus of divine communication -- "I will meet with you there and I will speak with you from above the kapporet" (v. 22). The kapporet thus unites atonement and revelation: the place where sin is covered is the place where God speaks. The LXX renders kapporet as hilastērion ("place of propitiation, mercy seat"), the same term Paul uses in Romans 3:25 when he describes Christ as the one "whom God set forth as a hilastērion through faith in His blood." The linguistic connection is direct: Christ is the antitype of the kapporet -- the place where divine justice and divine mercy meet.

**keruvim** (vv. 18–20) -- "cherubim." The Hebrew keruv (plural keruvim) designates the composite winged figures placed on either end of the kapporet. Their precise form is not described here -- the text assumes Moses knows what they look like, either from the heavenly pattern or from common ancient Near Eastern iconography. They are not the plump infants of Renaissance art but fearsome composite beings: in Ezekiel 10, the cherubim have four faces (human, lion, ox, eagle) and four wings. In Genesis 3:24, cherubim guard the way to the tree of life. Here they serve as throne-guardians: their wings spread upward and their faces look toward the kapporet, creating a throne-space above the Ark from which YHWH speaks. The Ark with its cherubim is effectively the footstool of God's invisible throne (cf. 1 Chr 28:2; Ps 99:1; 132:7-8). The LXX renders keruvim as cheroubim (a direct transliteration), and the Vaticanus text preserves this throughout.

**lechem panim** (v. 30) -- "bread of the Presence" (literally, "bread of faces/face"). The phrase has been variously translated: "showbread" (KJV), "bread of the Presence" (ESV, NASB), "bread set before the Lord." The noun panim ("face, faces, presence") indicates that this bread is placed "before the face of" YHWH -- it sits perpetually in the divine presence. Twelve loaves were set out every Sabbath (Lev 24:5-9), corresponding to the twelve tribes of Israel. The bread represents Israel's perpetual presentation before God and God's perpetual provision for Israel. The LXX renders lechem panim as artous enōpious ("bread of presence/facing") or artous tēs prothēseōs ("bread of the setting-forth"), the latter becoming the standard term in the New Testament (Matt 12:4; Heb 9:2).

**menorah** (vv. 31–39) -- "lampstand." The menorah (from the root n-w-r, "to give light") is described with extraordinary botanical detail: it has a central shaft, six branches, almond-shaped cups (gevi'im meshukqadim), buds (kaftorim), and blossoms (perachim). The tree-like design is not merely decorative but deeply symbolic: the menorah is an arboreal image, a stylized tree of light. Its almond motifs are significant -- the almond (shaqed) is the first tree to bloom in spring, and the Hebrew root sh-q-d also means "to watch, be vigilant" (cf. Jer 1:11-12, where God makes a wordplay: "I see a branch of an almond tree [shaqed]... I am watching [shoqed] over My word"). The seven-branched menorah of pure gold, hammered from a single talent (kikkar, approximately 75 pounds / 34 kg), is one piece (miqshah echad) -- not assembled from parts but beaten from a single mass of gold. The LXX renders menorah as luchnia ("lampstand"), and miqshah as toreutē ("of turned/lathe-worked metal"), emphasizing the craftsmanship.

**miqshah** (vv. 18, 31, 36) -- "hammered work, beaten work." The noun miqshah (from q-sh-h, "to be hard" or from n-q-sh, "to hammer") denotes metalwork produced by hammering a single piece of metal into shape -- repoussé work rather than casting. Both the cherubim and the menorah are to be made miqshah: hammered from one continuous piece of gold rather than assembled from separately cast components. This technique requires extraordinary skill and ensures structural unity -- the cherubim are not attachments to the kapporet but extensions of it; the branches of the menorah are not soldered to the shaft but beaten out from the same mass of gold. The theological symbolism of organic unity -- everything emerging from one source -- is implicit in the construction method.

### Grammatical Notes

**veyiqchu li terumah** (v. 2) -- "and let them take for Me an offering." The verb yiqchu is Qal jussive third person plural of l-q-ch ("to take"), expressing a command: "let them take." The preposition li ("for Me") indicates that the offering belongs to YHWH once given -- it is not a donation to the human builders but a sacred contribution to God. The verb "take" (laqach) rather than "give" (natan) is unexpected: one might expect "let them give Me an offering." The use of "take" frames the act from the perspective of the collectors rather than the donors, and may also imply that the offering is "taken" from what already belongs to God -- a return of His own gifts. The LXX renders this as kai labete moi aparchas ("and take for Me first-fruits").

**asher yiddevennu libbo** (v. 2) -- "whose heart moves/impels him." The verb yiddevennu is Qal imperfect third masculine singular of n-d-v ("to be willing, volunteer, incite") with a third masculine singular pronominal suffix. The root nadav denotes spontaneous, uncoerced generosity -- it is the root of nedavah ("freewill offering") and the personal name Nadav (Nadab). The heart (lev) is the seat of volition and decision in Hebrew anthropology. The phrase establishes the foundational principle for sacred giving: it must arise from the free movement of the heart, not from external compulsion. This same principle governs the actual collection in 35:5, 21, and Paul echoes it in 2 Corinthians 9:7 ("God loves a cheerful giver").

**ve'asu li miqdash veshakhanti betokham** (v. 8) -- The conjunction of two vav-consecutive clauses creates a purpose/result sequence: "Let them make for Me a sanctuary, and [as a result] I will dwell in their midst." The imperfect veshakhanti (from shakan, "to dwell") with vav-consecutive following the imperative ve'asu expresses the divine consequence of human obedience: if they build, He will dwell. The verb shakan is semantically rich: it means not merely "to reside" but "to settle, to make one's home" -- it connotes permanence and intimacy. The preposition betokham ("in their midst, among them") is spatially centered: God will dwell not at the periphery but at the center of the camp, as the Tabernacle's placement in the middle of the Israelite encampment (Num 2) will literalize.

**miqshah ta'aseh otam mishshney qetsot hakkapporet** (v. 19) -- The syntax is compressed. Miqshah ("hammered work") is an adverbial accusative specifying the manner of construction. The phrase mishshney qetsot hakkapporet ("from the two ends of the mercy seat") specifies that the cherubim are to be hammered out from the kapporet itself -- not made separately and attached, but formed from the same piece of gold. The min- preposition ("from") is partitive: the cherubim emerge from the kapporet as extensions of it. This detail is theologically significant: the guardians of the divine presence are organically part of the atonement cover, not additions to it.

**ure'eh va'aseh betavnitam asher attah mar'eh bahar** (v. 40) -- "And see and make according to their pattern which you are being shown on the mountain." The verb re'eh (Qal imperative of r-'a-h, "to see") paired with aseh (Qal imperative of '-s-h, "to make") creates a see-and-do sequence: Moses must first perceive the heavenly pattern, then reproduce it. The Hophal participle mar'eh ("being shown, being caused to see") indicates that the vision is initiated by God, not sought by Moses -- God causes Moses to see the tavnit. The prepositional phrase bahar ("on the mountain") anchors the heavenly vision to a specific earthly location, maintaining the paradox that Sinai is both an earthly mountain and the threshold of heaven.

### Structural Notes

Exodus 25 inaugurates the great Tabernacle instruction section (chs. 25-31), which constitutes one of the longest divine speeches in the Torah. This section is framed by the narrative of chapters 24 (Moses ascending into the glory-cloud) and chapter 32 (the golden calf crisis), creating a dramatic structural contrast: while God is giving Moses the blueprint for His authorized dwelling-place, the people at the base of the mountain are constructing an unauthorized image. The Tabernacle instructions and the golden calf episode are thus set in deliberate tension -- divine order versus human disorder, true worship versus false worship.

Chapter 25 itself has a clear tripartite structure, moving from the general to the specific: (1) the call for offerings and the statement of purpose (vv. 1-9), (2) the three items of Tabernacle furniture described in order from the most holy to the less holy -- the Ark (vv. 10-22), the Table (vv. 23-30), and the Lampstand (vv. 31-39) -- and (3) the concluding command to build according to the heavenly pattern (v. 40). The chapter moves from outside in (the people's offerings) and then from inside out (the Ark in the Most Holy Place, the Table and Lampstand in the Holy Place). The spatial logic is significant: God begins His instructions with the innermost and most sacred object (the Ark), then moves outward -- the reverse of how a worshiper would approach, but the natural order from God's perspective, beginning with His own throne.

The inclusio formed by tavnit in verses 9 and 40 frames the entire chapter as an exercise in faithful reproduction: everything that follows is to be built "according to the pattern." This framing device establishes that the Tabernacle is not a human invention but a divine design -- Moses is a copyist, not an architect. The earthly structure is derivative of the heavenly original, a theme that will reach its fullest theological expression in the letter to the Hebrews.

### Theological Notes

The opening command of Exodus 25 -- "Let them take for Me an offering" -- establishes the theological foundation for the entire Tabernacle project: God's dwelling among His people is funded by the free-will generosity of the people themselves. The Tabernacle is not imposed by divine fiat apart from human participation; it requires the collaboration of willing hearts. The phrase asher yiddevennu libbo ("whose heart moves him") makes voluntary internal motivation the prerequisite for sacred giving. This is remarkable in an ancient Near Eastern context where temple construction was typically the prerogative and obligation of kings (cf. the building inscriptions of Mesopotamian and Egyptian monarchs). In Israel, the sanctuary is built not by royal compulsion but by popular generosity -- and not merely by any popular generosity, but specifically by the generosity of the willing heart. The democratization of sacred building is itself a theological statement: the dwelling-place of God is the responsibility and privilege of the entire community.

Verse 8 is the theological center of the chapter and one of the most consequential verses in the Hebrew Bible: "Let them make for Me a sanctuary, and I will dwell in their midst." The verb shakan ("to dwell") introduces the concept that will define Israel's worship for a millennium: the God who created the cosmos and who manifested Himself in fire and cloud on Sinai now proposes to take up residence among His people in a portable tent. The scandal of this condescension is best appreciated against the background of Solomon's prayer at the temple dedication: "Will God indeed dwell on the earth? Behold, heaven and the highest heaven cannot contain You; how much less this house that I have built!" (1 Kgs 8:27). The paradox is already present in Exodus 25:8 -- the infinite God will dwell in a finite structure, the transcendent Creator will tabernacle in a tent of acacia wood and gold. This is the theological trajectory that reaches its climax in John 1:14: "The Word became flesh and dwelt [eskēnōsen, literally 'tabernacled'] among us."

The description of the Ark (vv. 10-22) reveals its dual function as throne and repository. As a repository, it contains the edut ("testimony") -- the stone tablets of the covenant (cf. 31:18; 40:20). As a throne, it bears the cherubim whose outspread wings form the seat of the invisible God (cf. 1 Sam 4:4, "YHWH of hosts, who is enthroned upon the cherubim"). The kapporet unites these functions: it is both the lid of the document box and the base of the divine throne, and it is the place where atonement blood is applied and where God speaks. The convergence of law, mercy, and revelation at a single point -- the kapporet -- is one of the most profound spatial-theological arrangements in all of Scripture. Where the law is stored (inside the Ark), mercy covers (the kapporet above), and God speaks (from between the cherubim) -- justice and mercy meet at the same location.

The menorah's botanical design (vv. 31-39) -- with its almond cups, buds, and blossoms -- makes it a stylized tree of light. Many scholars have seen in the menorah an echo of the tree of life in Eden (Gen 2:9; 3:22-24), now relocated within the sanctuary as a sign that the Tabernacle is a restoration of Edenic communion between God and humanity. The cherubim who guarded the tree of life in Genesis 3:24 now stand atop the Ark in the Most Holy Place; the tree of light (menorah) stands in the Holy Place before the veil. The Tabernacle recapitulates Eden: it is the place where God walks among His people (cf. Lev 26:12, using the same hithalekh as Gen 3:8), where the tree of life is symbolically present, and where the cherubim stand guard. What was lost in the garden is provisionally recovered in the sanctuary.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 25 (pages 67-68) preserves the LXX version with several notable features. At v. 2, the LXX renders terumah as aparchas ("first-fruits/first-portions"), interpreting the offering as a dedication of the best. At v. 7, the LXX clarifies the setting stones as lithous eis tēn glyphēn ("stones for engraving") and specifies their use for the ephod and the podērē (the full-length robe), using podērēs rather than a direct translation of choshen ("breastpiece"). At v. 8, the theologically significant veshakhanti betokham is rendered kai ophthēsomai en humin ("and I will appear among you"), shifting the metaphor from dwelling to appearing -- a more transcendent rendering that may reflect later theological sensibility about the nature of divine presence. At v. 9, tavnit is rendered paradeigma ("pattern, paradigm"), emphasizing the exemplary nature of the heavenly model. At v. 16, the LXX adds ta marturia ("the testimonies") for ha'edut ("the testimony"), using the same term (marturion) that becomes the standard LXX designation for the Tabernacle itself (hē skēnē tou marturiou, "the tent of testimony"). At v. 17, kapporet is rendered hilastērion ("propitiatory/mercy seat"), the term that will carry enormous theological weight in Romans 3:25. At vv. 18-20, the LXX's description of the cherubim closely follows the MT, with cheroubim as a direct transliteration. At v. 22, the LXX renders veno'adti lekha sham as kai gnōsthēsomai soi ekeithen ("and I will make Myself known to you from there"), interpreting the meeting as an act of self-revelation rather than merely an encounter. At v. 29, the LXX renders the vessel terminology with precision: trublia ("dishes"), thuiskas ("censers/incense vessels"), and spondeia ("libation vessels"). At v. 30, lechem panim is rendered artous enōpious ("bread of presence/facing"). At vv. 31-39, the menorah description follows the MT closely, with luchnia for menorah and the botanical terminology rendered with care. At v. 40, the concluding command to build according to the pattern shown on the mountain is rendered hora poiēseis kata ton tupon ton dedeigmenon soi en tō orei ("see that you make according to the type/pattern shown to you on the mountain"), using tupos -- the term the author of Hebrews will quote. The Vaticanus manuscript shows clear chapter division markers, and the uncial script is legible across all three columns on both pages, with the text of Exodus 25 beginning in the latter portion of page 67 and continuing through page 68.

--- LOGOS
